skill.name: spawn
skill.description: Spawn AI coding agents and generic CLI commands in separate terminal sessions for interactive delegation, authentication, and long-running work.
skill.summary: Launch AI coding agents or CLI commands in separate terminal sessions with optional output capture.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified extensive external command execution and filesystem use. The behavior matches the skill purpose, but raw command strings are passed through shell contexts and several cookbooks recommend bypassing approvals or sandboxing. No evidence found of prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration, so this is high risk rather than blocked critical risk.

high_findings.title: Raw Shell Command Execution
high_findings.description: The fork_terminal helper accepts a command string and injects it into shell, tmux, PowerShell, WSL, or osascript execution paths. This is expected for the skill, but it can execute arbitrary user-controlled commands with the caller privileges.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The function is explicitly designed to run caller-supplied command strings, and several branches use shell=True or shell command interpolation. This is a confirmed execution surface, although it is the stated purpose of the skill.

high_findings.title: Approval and Sandbox Bypass Recommended
high_findings.description: Multiple agent cookbooks instruct users to run AI coding tools with flags that bypass approvals, force tool use, or grant broad automatic action permission. This can allow spawned agents to edit files and execute commands without meaningful confirmation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky flags are documented as recommended defaults across several providers. The exact effect depends on each CLI, but the intent is clear automatic approval or sandbox bypass.

medium_findings.title: Temporary Output Files May Expose Sensitive Session Data
medium_findings.description: The helper writes clean agent output, debug logs, done markers, and generated scripts under /tmp. Captured output from AI agents or CLI commands can include source code, secrets, prompts, or authentication messages, and no restrictive file permissions are set.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly writes and reads temporary output files. Exposure depends on host permissions and output contents, so the risk is real but context-dependent.

medium_findings.title: Documentation Includes API Key and Network-Capable CLI Use
medium_findings.description: The Cursor cookbook documents API key use and the terminal skill supports arbitrary commands such as curl. The evidence is documentation rather than credential exfiltration code, but it increases the care required when publishing the skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API key references are legitimate CLI documentation and no exfiltration endpoint was found. The risk is medium because arbitrary spawned commands can still access network tools and local credentials.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto Signals Appear to be Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithms in markdown cookbooks, but review found model names, command examples, and option text rather than cryptographic implementation. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, or insecure crypto use in executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptive markdown or section headings, not crypto code. This makes the weak-crypto classification highly likely to be a scanner false positive.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found in Reviewed Files
low_findings.description: Targeted review for override language, fake system messages, pre-approval claims, and instructions to skip analysis found no evidence of prompt injection in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed instruction files contain mandatory workflow language, but it is scoped to using the skill and does not claim authority over security review. Confidence is limited because only repository files listed in the report were reviewed.

dangerous_patterns.title: shell=True with Caller-Supplied Command
dangerous_patterns.description: Linux fallback paths pass command strings to subprocess.Popen with shell=True. This is dangerous if prompts or user input are incorporated without strict quoting or user confirmation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command parameter is directly used in shell=True subprocess calls. This is a classic command injection and arbitrary execution pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Interactive Agent Launches with Broad Permissions
dangerous_patterns.description: Cookbook examples recommend permission bypass flags for Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, and Copilot. These defaults can amplify a malicious or mistaken prompt.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly show high-permission launch flags. The impact depends on the spawned agent and workspace, but the pattern is clearly high risk.

content.user_title: Spawn AI Agents and Terminal Commands
content.value_statement: Developers often need a separate terminal for interactive agents, authentication, or long-running CLI work. This skill provides structured instructions for launching those sessions and capturing useful output.
actual_capabilities: Launch Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, OpenCode, or Copilot CLI sessions in a separate terminal
Spawn generic CLI commands such as build tools, media tools, scripts, and network utilities
Provide provider-specific command patterns for interactive and non-interactive agent sessions
Capture agent output or debug logs to temporary files for later review
Open authentication flows for supported AI provider CLIs when login is required
Guide users to check command help before launching full terminal commands
limitations: It depends on locally installed CLI tools, terminal applications, tmux, or Windows Terminal.
It can run powerful commands with the same permissions as the current user.
Output capture can store sensitive command or agent output in temporary files.
It does not validate that a requested command is safe before execution.

use_cases.title: Start an interactive AI coding session
use_cases.description: Open a separate terminal for Claude Code, Codex, or another coding agent while keeping the main conversation available.

use_cases.title: Run a long CLI task outside the main session
use_cases.description: Launch build, media, or script commands that need interactive input or should continue independently.

use_cases.title: Handle provider login flows
use_cases.description: Open a terminal for CLI authentication when an AI provider requires browser or account login.

prompt_templates.title: Open a default coding agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a separate agent terminal
prompt_templates.prompt: Spawn an agent to inspect the current project and summarize the main components.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a specific provider
prompt_templates.scenario: Use Codex for a focused implementation task
prompt_templates.prompt: Spawn Codex in a new terminal to investigate the failing tests and suggest the smallest fix.

prompt_templates.title: Run a non-agent CLI command
prompt_templates.scenario: Start a long-running tool in another terminal
prompt_templates.prompt: Spawn a terminal to run the project build command after checking the command help output.

prompt_templates.title: Capture an agent result for review
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with later output retrieval
prompt_templates.prompt: Spawn Claude Code with a summary of this conversation, ask it to review the migration plan, and capture the clean output.

output_examples.input: Spawn Claude Code to review the current branch.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects the Claude Code cookbook, checks the CLI help, opens a new terminal, and returns the temporary output path if capture is enabled.

output_examples.input: Open a terminal for npm run build.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms this is a generic CLI task, reviews the command help where available, and launches it in a separate terminal session.

output_examples.input: Spawn Gemini with a summary of our work so far.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares a concise conversation summary, follows the Gemini cookbook, and starts an interactive Gemini session with the summary as context.

best_practices: Use the lowest permission mode that still supports the task.
Review command help and the final command before launching a terminal.
Disable output logging when prompts or command output may contain secrets.
anti_patterns: Do not run untrusted prompts with approval bypass or full system access.
Do not pass secrets in prompts or command arguments that may be logged.
Do not use this skill for simple non-interactive tasks that can run safely in the current session.

faq.question: Does this skill install AI agent CLIs?
faq.answer: No. It assumes the required CLIs are already installed and authenticated on the local machine.

faq.question: Can it run any terminal command?
faq.answer: Yes, it is designed for arbitrary CLI commands, so users must review commands carefully before execution.

faq.question: Does it support Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes cookbooks for Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, OpenCode, and Copilot.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk high?
faq.answer: The skill launches external processes and recommends powerful approval bypass modes for several coding agents.

faq.question: Where does captured output go?
faq.answer: The helper writes captured agent output or debug logs to temporary files when logging is enabled.

faq.question: Is this best for automated multi-agent workflows?
faq.answer: No. It is better for interactive terminals. Native task invocation is usually better for automation.
